moment_text: So Mo More recently it was with the passing of my mom, but I've been focusing on my mental health for what feels like a long time now with my high pressure job. I really saw the importance of it as it started to affect my home life. So then when my mom passed away, I really needed to refocus. You know, I started to see honestly what really mattered how. I could take care of myself. You know, I started, I started painting. You know, I, if you see my house, there is coloring books everywhere. I have like every art supply you can imagine. And. I just sat down one day and just started to put that pain on the canvas. My focus like instantly changed all my emotions, all my feelings. I, I just got out on that canvas and I'm like gonna cry 'cause it makes me emotional, don't get me wrong. Going back and forth from the hospital. I would scream in the car. I was angry. I felt all the emotions, but just being able to put that on that canvas was just a totally different feeling.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains agency signals (seeking_support) as the speaker actively uses art to process grief and other strong emotions.

moment_text: Honestly, it felt amazing. You know, coloring was something my mom and I did from a very young age where, you know, we'd go to the food store, the dollar store, wherever, and we were always picking up coloring books. You know, we'd sit at the kitchen table and we would just color. It just felt natural to kind of go back to that during my, my grieving process. Yeah, and it just felt natural to just sit down and just put it on that canvas. You know, you can use every color and it depicts how you're feeling. Mine, you know, mostly are bright colors, you know, 'cause I. Don't wanna always feel that sadness, you know, that was kind of through that whole process, but just getting it out there and,
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains agency signals (seeking_support) and emotional signals within a family system interaction context.
notes_for_reviewer: The moment connects a childhood activity with her mother to her current grieving process, making it a cross_life_stage experience.

moment_text: Sure. So my husband has been my number one supporter from the beginning. You know, he was the one, he was like, I, this is beautiful, like you should share it. And I was like, I don't think so. Like, I don't know what you're talking about. Like I have no training. Like I never went to art school again. It was just something that I did for myself. So my husband started to share it with some of our friends and they were like, wow, this is really good. And then my friends, I would give them as gifts for birthdays or holidays. And then some of my other friends were like, can you do specific colors? And I was like, yeah, sure. And like. So many of them have them in their homes. Anybody and everybody can see them.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains barrier signals (fear_of_judgment), agency signals (sharing_story), and emotional signals (self_doubt, validation) related to sharing her art.

moment_text: I met this person when my husband and I were on a date and they were like, my husband shared it with them 'cause they did tie ding and he was like, wow, like I'd like to carry some of these in my shop. You know, went from there. Like I just needed that first person outside of my group that was like. These are really good. We have a local shop here who carries a few of them. I have a bookshop, another local spot that has them up for the month of June for pride, you know, 'cause of all the colors and rainbows. And then I just started getting into like festivals, different art festivals again. Like it's only been like the last year that I've really been doing this, but they found their home.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains barrier signals (lack_of_validation) and agency signals (sharing_story) as the speaker gains confidence through community recognition.

moment_text: It is when that person comes up, like I'm getting goosebumps right now just talking about it. You know, that's it. Like I love creating them and all the different ones and I've since branched out to different shape shapes and stuff. Like, I started with circles 'cause I was like, circles are awesome. I've never seen circle canvases. And then just kind of where my mind just has, you know, gone. moment_text: Anything bright, you know, just, it gives me that like visceral response where it just puts a smile on my face. There are times where I'm not, you know, happy and I don't wanna get out of bed, but then I like, look, I have a wall outside my bathroom that is just full of prints from artists all over that have positive affirmations on there. So I've just kind of tried to cultivate that, that positive atmosphere.

moment_text: Absolutely. You know, I've been in therapy for years and I love it. I've had different therapists, I've journaled, I've meditated, you know, I've done all different things and I find comfort in different ways, but I always kind of go back to that art. You'll see my, my art table's not very big, but when my mental health is, I don't wanna say struggling because it's not always when it's. Something bad. It could be. When it's something good, it is just full of pieces in progress and it does, it just kind of helps me get it out and I'm dealing, like you said, dealing with the loss of my mom, changing careers after.

moment_text: Most of my life, kind of trying to refocus and figuring out a way to help that, try to get outside and go for walks because you know, like you said, like caregiving, like I am my grandmom's caregiver and have been. For, for like the last six years, you know? And that's been a lot. I mean, I wouldn't change it for the world and I'm super grateful to have support, you know, from my husband, my brother, my daughter. But it, it does, it takes a toll. 'cause you know, I have a family, I have a full-time job and I also have, have to take care of her. She's wonderful. We're kind of transitioning to, you know, the different stage in her life and kind of figuring all that out. Therapy and, you know, working on my mental health, you know, I need these, I need to take care of myself. And that is one thing that my one therapist told me years ago, and I, we talk about it in this house, my, we all use it is making sure your batteries are charged. Because if your batteries aren't charged, then you are no use to yourself and to anybody around you. You need to take care of yourself first and how important that is. And I wasn't always the case. But once I really started focusing on myself and my mental health, I can do these things. They're not always easy. I'm able to, to do it and take up, you know, take that space and take that time, but also take that time for myself.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains strong barrier signals (time_constraint), agency signals (setting_boundaries), and emotional signals (overwhelm) related to caregiver burnout.

moment_text: My self care and figuring out and like what is important, setting boundaries, learning to say no. That was something I struggle with, as you mentioned, a caregiver. moment_text: I want people to know that it's possible. You know, you, you don't have to be stuck. I felt like I was stuck for. You know, almost like 20 years felt like that, you know? And so I would believe wholeheartedly and lose the imposter syndrome and just. Derive so much joy from it and it'll keep, it'll manifest.
